title: Proceedings
description: The Independent Forum for the Sea Services since 1873.
text:
Ep. 483: Answering for Atrocities
Our new Naval History Editor-in-Chief Emily Abdow talks with Michael Eastman about the lessons and legacy of the U.S. Navy’s 1945–49 War Crimes Program in the Pacific
CEO Notes
Upcoming events, new content from the Naval Institute Press, and a reminder for members to cast their vote in the 2026 Board election.
2025 in Review: A Year of Enormous Changes at Sea
This issue features the winners of the 2025 General Prize Essay Contest. Special thanks to Andrew and Barbara Taylor for their enduring support for this contest.
U.S. Navy Year in Review
By Robert D. Holzer and Lieutenant Commander Andy Cichon, U.S. Navy (Retired)
As the U.S. Navy confronted the demands of ongoing naval combat and the need to reset the fleet for great power naval competition, 2025 was another year of near-constant action.
U.S. Marine Corps Year in Review
By Lieutenant Colonel James W. Hammond III, U.S. Marine Corps (Retired)
In 2025, Marines celebrated the legacy of global responsiveness and lethal combined arms passed down from the Marine Corps’ 1775 founding in Philadelphia.
U.S. Coast Guard Year in Review
By Blair Sweigart and Joe DiRenzo
For the Coast Guard, 2025 was a year like no other: organizational transformation, historic investment in people and platforms, and significant operational outcomes.
U.S. Naval Aviation & Weapons Year in Review
By Captain Matt Wright, U.S. Navy
While 2025 saw significant operational success for naval aviation, there were several high-profile “others” to go along with the many “goods.”
Global Maritime Industry Year in Review
By Dr. Shashi Kumar
It became more complex in 2025 to own and operate an internationally trading commercial ship.
NOAA Year in Review
By Rear Admiral Tim Gallaudet, U.S. Navy (Retired)
As 2026 unfolds, there is much debate about the future of NOAA under the second Trump administration.
Thoughts on Numbered Fleet Command
By Vice Admiral Fred Kacher, U.S. Navy
Among the senior mentors from whom I have learned the most in the Navy, most describe numbered fleet command as the best job they ever had.
A New (Old) Call to Service
By William Kiel
Today, as war clouds threaten in multiple theaters and military deployments grow in frequency and length, the Temporary Reserve could be another option for Americans to serve.
